title: The Sentiment Classification Task
concept: Sentiment classification flags the emotional tone of text, commonly used to monitor customer reviews and social media feeds.

exercises.prompt: What does "sentiment" refer to in sentiment classification refer to? What does "email"

options.text: The emotional tone of text (positive, negative, neutral)
The total number of words in a sentence
The font size used in an email

exercises.explain: Sentiment classification is all about identifying the emotional tone—such as positive, negative, or neutral—in written text.

cards.title: Can AI Read Our Feelings?
cards.body: Every day, millions of tweets, reviews, and comments are posted online. How do companies know if people love or hate their products without reading every single post manually? They use AI to read the room!

cards.title: What is Sentiment Classification?
cards.body: Sentiment classification is a machine learning task that automatically scans text to identify the emotional tone behind it—categorizing it as positive, negative, or neutral.

cards.title: The Digital Mailroom Clerk
cards.body: Imagine a mailroom clerk who instantly reads incoming letters and places them into three colored bins: green for love letters (positive), red for complaints (negative), and yellow for basic updates (neutral). Sentiment classification is like this clerk, but reading thousands of digital messages in a second.

cards.title: Real-World App Reviews
cards.body: Businesses use sentiment classifiers to read thousands of online reviews. If a customer writes, "This app crashes constantly!", the AI flags it as 'Negative' so developers can fix the bug immediately.

cards.title: It is Not Just a Keyword Search
cards.body: You might think AI only looks for simple words like "good" or "bad". In reality, modern classifiers look at context. They can understand that "Not bad at all!" is actually a positive statement, even though it contains the word "bad".

cards.title: Sorting the Tone
cards.body: See how the classifier groups different user reviews based on the emotions expressed in the text.

left.text: "The food was cold and arrived late."

exercises.prompt: If a classifier reads the review: "This toy broke on day one!", what category will it most likely choose?
exercises.explain: Feedback like 'This toy broke on day one!' expresses frustration and disappointment, making it a negative sentiment.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Sentiment classifiers can only label text as Positive or Negative, nothing else.
exercises.explain: Classifiers can also sort text into 'Neutral' when a statement is just a fact without any strong positive or negative feelings, like 'The box is blue.'

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the goal of sentiment analysis.
exercises.sentence: Sentiment classification is used to find the emotional ______ of a text.
exercises.explain: Sentiment classification focuses on identifying the underlying emotional tone (positive, negative, or neutral) rather than just checking grammar or spelling.

exercises.prompt: Which of these statements shows a clear NEGATIVE sentiment that an AI would flag?

options.text: I bought a new book today.
This is the worst app ever!

exercises.explain: While 'I bought a new book' is neutral, 'This is the worst app ever' is clearly a negative human sentiment. The AI text 'I am a computer program' is a factual statement without emotional tone.

exercises.prompt: A customer writes: "The service was slow, but the food made up for it." What is a sentiment classifier trying to do when reading this?

options.text: It counts how many times the word 'happy' appears.
It evaluates the overall emotional tone expressed in the text.
It corrects spelling mistakes and grammar in a review.
It translates text from one language to another.

exercises.explain: Sentiment classification looks at the overall emotional tone of the text, not just single words in isolation. A review can say 'This phone is not bad!' which contains the negative word 'bad' but actually expresses a positive opinion.
